channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Xi Is Poised to Press Trump on Arms Sales to Taiwan During China Summit
item.description: Beijing has called Taiwan the “core of China’s core interests.” Xi Jinping is likely to focus on getting President Trump to slow approval of more weapons for the self-governing island.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:credit: Ann Wang/Reuters
item.media:description: A high mobility artillery rocket system, or HIMARS, displayed during a military exercise in Taichung, Taiwan, last year.

item.title: China Seeks A.I. Independence, Weakening Trump’s Leverage
item.description: Before this week’s U.S.-Chinese summit, Beijing reached a milestone in its quest for technological self-sufficiency.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: DeepSeek said for the first time that its new model had been optimized to run on chips made by the Chinese tech giant Huawei.

item.title: China Increasingly Views Trump’s America as an Empire in Decline
item.description: For decades, many Chinese viewed the United States with a mix of admiration, envy and resentment. President Trump’s volatile second term shattered that image.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: The port at Tianjin, China. In response to President Trump’s tariffs, China has become more assertive on the global stage.

item.title: China Sought Access to Anthropic’s Newest A.I. The Answer Was No.
item.description: The latest artificial intelligence models from Anthropic and OpenAI are extending the United States’ lead over China and intensifying the rivalry between the countries.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz, Julian E. Barnes, Sheera Frenkel and Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Marissa Leshnov for The New York Times
item.media:description: A growing number of national security officials and analysts have begun to liken the intensifying competition between China and the United States over artificial intelligence to the Cold War’s nuclear arms race.

item.title: How Iran’s Internet Blackout Is Crippling Its Economy, and the Fight Over Fruit-Flavored Vapes
item.description: Plus, how everything became a subscription.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Yeganeh Torbati and Sopan Deb
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iran was already struggling economically before 2026 brought widespread instability. A government-imposed internet shutdown has crippled an entire sector.

item.title: Who Might Challenge Starmer in a Leadership Contest?
item.description: Health Secretary Wes Streeting, Mayor of Greater Manchester Andy Burnham and former deputy prime minister Angela Rayner are among possible contenders.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle

item.title: As Trump Dismisses Iran Truce Plan, World Braces for Lasting Economic Pain
item.description: President Trump said the fragile cease-fire was on “life support,” and India’s leader urged residents to cut back on purchases and travel.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Sanam Mahoozi, Michael Levenson and Max Bearak
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran last week. Iranian officials are facing domestic pressure to reach a deal as the country’s economic problems have grown only more dire since the war.

item.title: Who Owns the Strait?
item.description: We take a look at the laws governing waterways.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran representing Iran’s hold on the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: How an ‘Impossible’ Idea Led to a Pancreatic Cancer Breakthrough
item.description: The new strategy also holds promise for lung and colon tumors. Here’s how scientists discovered it.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata and Rebecca Robbins
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: How to Eat Through Our List of the Best Restaurants in New York City
item.description: Don’t have the time (or money) to try all 100? Here are a dozen bite-size bucket lists to inspire you.
item.dc:creator: Nikita Richardson, Becky Hughes, Luke Fortney, Mahira Rivers, Ryan Sutton, Eric Asimov, Ligaya Mishan and Rozalina Burkova

item.title: Steel Tariffs Are Raising the Price of Canned Foods
item.description: The cost to put food in cans remains high because manufacturers must still import steel from overseas. U.S. Steel plans to reopen a tin-plate factory.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Ross Mantle
item.media:description: Rolls of tin-plated steel at Ohio Coatings in Yorkville, Ohio.

item.title: Kari Lake, Who Led Effort to Shutter Voice of America, Is Named Ambassador to Jamaica
item.description: Ms. Lake tried and failed to fire hundreds of Voice of America journalists and to cut funding for other federally funded news groups like Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Agency for Global Media
item.media:credit: Maansi Srivastava for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kari Lake speaking at the annual CPAC event in Oxon Hill, Md., last year.

item.title: RFK Jr. Is Driving a Vast Inquiry Into Vaccines, Despite His Public Silence
item.description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has toned down his public criticism of vaccines, under orders from the White House. But inside his department, a sprawling research effort is a top priority.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Russia Keeps Attacking U.S. Firms in Ukraine. The White House Is Silent.
item.description: Facilities tied to Coca-Cola, Cargill, Mondelez and others appear to have been deliberately hit. The Trump administration’s muted response has raised concerns.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:credit: Viktoriia Yakymenko/Global Images Ukraine, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A rescue worker at a Philip Morris factory in Kharkiv, Ukraine, which was hit in a Russian missile strike in January.

item.title: The War in Iran Is Another Blow to Russia’s Credibility. Or Is It?
item.description: Despite its long slog in Ukraine and the loss of former allies like Syria, Russia is benefiting from costly energy, a clever narrative and America’s own failure to achieve a quick victory.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Maxim Shipenkov
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia at the Kremlin in Moscow, on Saturday.

item.title: Former Congressman Barney Frank, Now in Hospice, Offers Democrats Frank Words of Advice
item.description: Mr. Frank speaks about the missteps of the Democratic Party and his hope for its future. “Frankly, if I weren’t dying, people wouldn’t be paying as much attention.”
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:credit: Greg Miller for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former Representative Barney Frank in Ogunquit, Maine, in 2017. Mr. Frank, who has congestive heart failure, is speaking out on the future of the Democratic Party.

item.title: What to Watch in Tuesday’s Primaries in Nebraska and West Virginia
item.description: Democrats will choose a candidate in a House battleground in Nebraska, and an unusual Senate primary could decide the fate of Omaha’s “blue dot” vote.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: Some people are concerned that a quirk in how Nebraska allocates its Electoral College votes, known as the “blue dot" system, could be abolished by Republicans.

item.title: Why So Many Guys Are Obsessed With Testosterone
item.description: From the Trump administration to online influencers, the hormone is increasingly seen as the key to achieving a new male ideal.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi

item.title: What to Know About the New Obsession With Testosterone
item.description: From politics to influencers and beyond, the hormone is being used not just for medical reasons but in pursuit of a new masculine ideal.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi

item.title: Wrongful Death Lawsuits Against OpenAI Test a New Strategy
item.description: The cases seek to use consumer product safety laws to rein in chatbot companies.
item.dc:creator: Kashmir Hill
item.media:credit: Ilana Panich-Linsman for The New York Times
item.media:description: After the death of her son, Sam Nelson, Leila Scott-Turner discovered his conversations with ChatGPT about using drugs.

item.title: Start-Up Raises $1.3 Billion for an A.I. ‘Grid’
item.description: As tech giants dominate the hardware needed to build artificial intelligence, Amp hopes to create an alternative.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anjney Midha founded Amp to create a pool of A.I. computer chips for start-ups, universities and other organizations.

item.title: Is France’s Centrist Leader Trying to Weatherproof It From the Far Right?
item.description: President Emmanuel Macron has appointed a string of allies who will remain in critical state positions after next year’s presidential election, and who could thwart a far-right successor.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Anthony Pizzoferrato/Associated Press
item.media:description: President Emmanuel Macron of France speaking in Yerevan, Armenia, last week. He cannot run for re-election in France’s next presidential election.

item.title: Did Epstein Actually Write That Suicide Note? Here’s How We Tried to Find Out.
item.description: Three New York Times reporters discuss the steps they have taken to learn more about a note found in Jeffrey Epstein’s jail cell in the weeks before his death.
item.dc:creator: Virginia Hughes

item.title: Altar Boy Who Called Out Sexual Abuse in the Philippines Is Now a Lawyer for Victims
item.description: Michal Gatchalian dared to speak out against sexual abuse by a priest in the devoutly Catholic Philippines. He’s now a lawyer helping other victims.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee, Aie Balagtas See and Jes Aznar
item.media:description: Michal Gatchalian last year in front of a makeshift altar he had setup at his suburban village’s clubhouse. He no longer goes to the church where he was an altar boy.

item.title: The Shared Feeling of Being Harvested by the Future
item.description: There is a shared sense of precarity that lies beneath the envy and distrust.
item.dc:creator: Yi-Ling Liu

item.title: We Should Be Taking Hantavirus More Seriously
item.description: There are too many unknowns to be certain about much.
item.dc:creator: Zeynep Tufekci
item.media:credit: Beatrice Salomone/Connected Archives

item.title: Stephen Colbert Hosts 4 of His ‘Best Television Friends’
item.description: Jimmy Kimmel, Jimmy Fallon, John Oliver and Seth Meyers, co-hosts of the Strike Force Five podcast along with Colbert, joined as guests on what is likely their last appearance together on the “Late Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Instructure Strikes Deal for Hackers for Return of Canvas Data
item.description: Instructure, which provides Canvas software to thousands of schools and universities around the world, did not say what it had given the hackers in exchange for the stolen data.
item.dc:creator: Qasim Nauman
item.media:credit: Alessandro Penso for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hackers stole data from Canvas, an education platform used by thousands of schools and universities around the world.

item.title: EBay Rejects GameStop’s $55 Billion Takeover Bid
item.description: The online marketplace eBay called the cash-and-stock proposal “neither credible nor attractive.”
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: The headquarters of eBay in San Jose, Calif. GameStop announced a proposal last week to combine with the company, which is nearly four times its size

item.title: Texas Rainbow Crosswalk Ban Leads San Antonio to Paint Sidewalks Instead
item.description: After the state ordered the removal of rainbow crosswalks, San Antonio used colorful sidewalks to keep public pride symbols alive in the city.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Christopher Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The rainbows on crosswalks began disappearing after the state ordered all Texas cities to remove the colorful crosswalks and similar markings, citing safety issues and opposition to political messaging.

item.title: Rex Reed, Film Critic Known for Acerbic Reviews, Dies at 87
item.description: He fawned over Old Hollywood stars and sparred with Frank Sinatra. Nora Ephron marveled at his ability to get his subjects to say the things they did.
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rex Reed in 2017. If he disliked someone or — worse — found the person merely uninteresting, it was wise to duck and cover.
